summary: The Hanbaliyya are the school (madhhab) named for Ahmad ibn Hanbal, one of the four surviving Sunni schools of law, distinguished by its insistence on grounding doctrine and practice in the Qur'an, the Sunna and the reports of the early community and its reserve toward speculative reasoning; historically it was also the principal carrier of Athari (traditionist) theology.
description: The Hanbaliyya take their name from Ahmad ibn Hanbal (164-241 AH / c. 780-855 CE), a Baghdad hadith master and jurist who became the emblem of the traditionist cause during the mihna, the caliphal inquisition (initiated 218/833) that sought to compel scholars to affirm the createdness of the Qur'an; his refusal, and the punishment he endured, secured his authority among the Ahl al-Hadith. Ahmad ibn Hanbal is best understood as a traditionist scholar of hadith rather than a systematic jurist, and the legal school bearing his name was consolidated by later followers, among them al-Khallal (d. 311/923), who collected his responsa, into one of the four Sunni madhhabs, alongside the Hanafi, Maliki and Shafi'i schools. In jurisprudence (fiqh) the school is characterized by its priority on the revealed texts and the practice of the Prophet's Companions, its acceptance even of weak hadith over analogical reasoning in some cases, and a corresponding caution toward juristic discretion (istihsan) and unrestrained analogy (qiyas). In theology the school became the principal institutional home of Atharism, the traditionist doctrine that affirms the revealed statements about God and His attributes 'without asking how' (bila kayf) and declines figurative reinterpretation (ta'wil); the association is strong enough that 'Hanbali' and 'Athari' are often used together, though they are not identical (see editorial notes). Hanbalism had a turbulent early history in Baghdad, the activist populism associated with al-Barbahari (d. 329/941) drew caliphal reproof, and it produced major later authorities including the jurist Ibn Qudama (d. 620/1223), author of the legal compendium al-Mughni, and the theologian-jurist Ibn Taymiyya (d. 728/1328) with his student Ibn al-Qayyim (d. 751/1350). Numerically the smallest of the four schools today, Hanbalism is concentrated in the Arabian Peninsula, and it furnished the juristic and, in part, the theological background against which the modern Salafi and Wahhabi movements developed.
editorial_notes: Jurisprudence-vs-theology distinction (central to this entry): the Hanbaliyya is primarily a school of law (madhhab / fiqh), whereas Atharism is a theological orientation; the entry keeps these separate. Historically most Hanbalis held Athari theology and Hanbalism was its main vehicle, but the two are not coextensive, some Hanbalis inclined toward kalam, and some adherents of the other law schools held traditionist theology. For that reason the theological content is described in the companion 'athariyya' entry, and this entry treats Hanbalism chiefly as the juristic school and historical carrier. Ahmad ibn Hanbal's own role is qualified: scholarship (e.g. Melchert) stresses that he was a traditionist and hadith scholar and that the systematized madhhab is substantially the work of later followers; the 'founded_by' relation should be read in that light and is glossed accordingly. Dates for Ahmad ibn Hanbal (164-241/780-855) are well attested; the school's 'founding' is dated only approximately to his lifetime and the mihna (see period note). The claim, sometimes made, that classical Hanbalism and modern Wahhabism are simply identical is contested in the scholarly literature (e.g. Michael Cook) and is not asserted here. Several relation targets (ibn-taymiyya, ibn-qudama, mihna, khalq-al-quran) are not yet separate entries in this dataset. Per the source-diversity principle, two primary Muslim creedal texts from within the school have been added at medium confidence, Ibn Qudāma's Lumʿat al-Iʿtiqād and the Uṣūl al-Sunna attributed to Aḥmad ibn Ḥanbal, as the tradition's own articulations of its creed (verified to exist and treat the school; the latter's attribution/recensions are noted as discussed in the scholarship). Per the source-quality principle (neutrality #9), the two reference/tertiary sources previously cited (Encyclopaedia Britannica and Wikipedia) have been removed; the entry retains two academic monographs (Melchert, Holtzman) and two primary creedal texts, so no replacement was needed.

period.note: Approximate. Ahmad ibn Hanbal lived 164-241/780-855; the school is dated here loosely to his lifetime and the mihna (from 218/833), though the madhhab as an organized legal school was consolidated by followers over the following generations. The school remains active, so no active_until is asserted.

relations.note: Eponym; a traditionist hadith master rather than a systematic jurist. The organized madhhab was consolidated by later followers such as al-Khallal (see editorial notes).
Ahmad ibn Hanbal (d. 241/855), the school's emblematic authority, made prominent by his stance during the mihna.
Ibn Taymiyya (d. 728/1328), major later Hanbali jurist and Athari theologian.
Ibn Qudama (d. 620/1223), Hanbali jurist and author of the legal compendium al-Mughni.

Rejects the createdness of the Qur'an; Ahmad ibn Hanbal's refusal to affirm it during the mihna is definitive for the school. (Relation expresses the school's position on this doctrine; the school denies createdness.)
Hanbalism was historically the principal vehicle of Athari (traditionist) theology, though the two are not identical.
The school grew out of, and remained close to, the traditionist Ahl al-Hadith milieu.
The school defined itself against Mu'tazili rationalism, above all over the createdness of the Qur'an during the mihna.

sources.citation: Melchert, Christopher. Ahmad ibn Hanbal (Makers of the Muslim World). Oneworld, 2006; and Melchert, The Formation of the Sunni Schools of Law, 9th-10th Centuries C.E. Brill, 1997.
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; not reproduced here.

sources.citation: Holtzman, Livnat. Anthropomorphism in Islam: The Challenge of Traditionalism (700-1350). Edinburgh University Press, 2018 (on Hanbali theology and its distinctiveness within Sunnism).
sources.license: Proprietary, cited for reference; not reproduced here.

sources.citation: Ibn Qudāma al-Maqdisī, Muwaffaq al-Dīn. Lumʿat al-Iʿtiqād al-Hādī ilā Sabīl al-Rashād (Sufficiency in Creed), 7th/13th c. A concise creed by a leading Ḥanbalī jurist, long a standard primer of Atharī–Ḥanbalī ʿaqīda covering the divine attributes, God's speech (kalām) and the uncreatedness of the Qurʾān, and qadar. Multiple English translations exist.
sources.license: Primary source; cited for reference, not reproduced.

sources.citation: Aḥmad ibn Ḥanbal. Uṣūl al-Sunna (The Foundations of the Sunna), 3rd/9th c. A creedal statement attributed to the school's eponym setting out the traditionist positions definitive for the madhhab, notably that the Qurʾān is the uncreated speech of God and the affirmation of the divine attributes as transmitted. Attribution and recensions are discussed in the scholarship; relevant text not read line-by-line in this pass.
sources.license: Primary source; cited for reference, not reproduced.
